title: Insurance and Risk Management
objectives: Describe basic business insurance requirements and coverage options for U.S. operations
Estimate insurance costs and identify which policies are needed before launch
Explain key regulatory exposure areas for international founders

contentBlocks.title: Shielding Your Business: Insurance and Risk Management
contentBlocks.content:
Now that you have locked down your intellectual property inside the launch facility, walk over to the safety and compliance wing. No business should operate without proper insurance and risk management.

A founder from Costa Rica lost $22,000 because he closed a deal with a handshake instead of a contract. In the U.S., that handshake is worth almost nothing — three months later, the distributor changed payment terms and refused to pay outstanding invoices. Without a signed contract, the founder had no legal leverage. This is why contracts matter (we cover contract types in the next lesson), but first, you need to understand the insurance and risk management foundations that protect your business every day.

Many international founders focus on the legal setup — entity formation, IP, contracts — and forget to guard against day-to-day business risks. One lawsuit without insurance coverage can shut you down. One slip-and-fall at your office, one product defect, one professional error that costs a client money. Insurance is your safety net for the risks you cannot predict.

contentBlocks.title: Business Insurance — What Coverage Do You Need?

cards.heading: General Liability
cards.desc: Required by most: Covers customer injuries, property damage, lawsuits. Get this FIRST.
cards.descEs: Requerido por la mayoría: Cubre lesiones a clientes, daños a propiedad, demandas. Obténlo PRIMERO.

cards.heading: Professional Liability / E&O
cards.desc: For service businesses: Covers mistakes, negligence claims, missed deadlines. Required if you give advice or provide professional services.
cards.descEs: Para negocios de servicios: Cubre errores, reclamos por negligencia, plazos incumplidos. Requerido si das asesoría o servicios profesionales.

cards.heading: Workers' Compensation
cards.desc: Required in Florida (4+ employees): Covers employee injuries on the job. Cost varies by industry and payroll. Mandatory — not optional.
cards.descEs: Requerido en Florida (4+ empleados): Cubre lesiones laborales. El costo varía por industria y nómina. Obligatorio — no opcional.

cards.heading: Commercial Property
cards.desc: If you have a physical space: Covers equipment, inventory, office contents. Includes fire, theft, some natural disasters.
cards.descEs: Si tienes un espacio físico: Cubre equipo, inventario, contenido de oficina. Incluye incendio, robo, algunos desastres naturales.

cards.heading: Business Owner's Policy / BOP
cards.desc: Best value for small businesses: Bundles general liability + property insurance at a discount. Great starting point.
cards.descEs: Mejor valor para pequeños negocios: Combina responsabilidad general + seguro de propiedad con descuento. Excelente punto de partida.

cards.heading: Cyber Liability
cards.desc: For any business handling customer data: Covers data breaches, ransomware, notification costs. Increasingly essential.
cards.descEs: Para cualquier negocio que maneje datos de clientes: Cubre violaciones de datos, ransomware, costos de notificación. Cada vez más esencial.

contentBlocks.footer: How to Buy: Get quotes from 3+ providers, use an independent insurance broker, review annually. Just starting? Get General Liability first. Adding employees? Add Workers' Comp. Handling data? Add Cyber Liability.
contentBlocks.footerEs: Cómo Comprar: Obtén cotizaciones de 3+ proveedores, usa un corredor de seguros independiente, revisa anualmente. ¿Recién empezando? Obtén Responsabilidad General primero. ¿Contratando empleados? Agrega Compensación al Trabajador. ¿Manejando datos? Agrega Responsabilidad Cibernética.

contentBlocks.title: Did You Know?
contentBlocks.content: Central Florida's Space Coast has hosted over 4,000 rocket launches since 1950, including every crewed NASA mission. The same culture of risk management that keeps rockets safe also drives the region's insurance industry: Florida has one of the largest property insurance markets in the country. For business founders, the parallel is clear — just as every launch requires a safety review, every business needs protection against the risks that could ground your operation.

contentBlocks.title: Business Insurance, What You Need and What Can Wait
contentBlocks.content:
Insurance in the U.S. is not optional. Some types are required by law. Others are required in practice, since clients and landlords demand proof.

General Liability Insurance (GL): Your most important policy. It covers third-party bodily injury, property damage, and advertising injury. Cost: $400–$800/year. Many landlords and B2B clients require proof before they will work with you.

Professional Liability / E&O: Covers claims your professional services caused financial harm — a software bug, bad advice, or a missed deadline. Cost: $500–$2,500/year.

Workers' Compensation: Required in Florida with four or more employees (one in construction). Cost varies by industry and payroll.

Commercial Property Insurance: Covers equipment, inventory, and office contents. Skip if working from home; get it for a physical office or warehouse.

A Nigerian founder found that U.S. vendor onboarding required proof of $1 million GL and $1 million E&O before clients would sign. Budget for insurance before you need it.

contentBlocks.title: Operating Without General Liability Insurance
contentBlocks.content: Here's where the risk is real. One slip-and-fall, one product issue, one bad contract — and you're personally exposed if your business doesn't have GL coverage. Even if your LLC provides liability protection, courts can "pierce the corporate veil" if you're operating without basic insurance. GL is affordable ($400–$800/year for most small businesses) and many landlords and B2B clients require it. Don't skip it.

contentBlocks.title: Insurance Types — What Each Policy Covers

sections.heading: General Liability Insurance
sections.content:
**Covers:** Third-party bodily injury, property damage, advertising injury.

**Cost:** $400–$1,500/year for small businesses.

**Who needs it:** Almost every business. Often required by landlords and contracts.

sections.heading: Professional Liability (E&O)
sections.content:
**Covers:** Claims of negligence, errors, or failure to deliver professional services.

**Cost:** $500–$3,000/year depending on industry.

**Who needs it:** Consultants, IT companies, any service-based business.

sections.heading: Workers' Compensation
sections.content:
**Covers:** Employee injuries and illnesses on the job.

**Cost:** Varies by industry and payroll size.

**Who needs it:** Required in Florida if you have 4+ employees (construction: 1+ employees).

sections.heading: Cyber Liability Insurance
sections.content:
**Covers:** Data breaches, ransomware, notification costs, legal defense.

**Cost:** $500–$5,000/year for small businesses.

**Who needs it:** Any business that stores customer data, processes payments, or relies on technology.

contentBlocks.title: Regulatory Exposure, Industries That Need Extra Attention
contentBlocks.content:
Some industries have additional rules you must follow before operating in the U.S. Regulatory agencies will not clear your business without the right permits, and the consequences of non-compliance are severe. Central Florida's economy spans aerospace, tourism, healthcare, technology, and agriculture, and each sector brings its own regulatory requirements.

Food and Beverage: FDA (federal) and FDACS (Florida) compliance — labeling, inspections, product testing. A Colombian coffee brand spent $8,000 on FDA-compliant labeling before listing on Amazon.

Healthcare and Wellness: HIPAA compliance for protected health information. Medical devices need FDA clearance. Telehealth faces state-by-state licensing.

Financial Services: SEC, FINRA, and state-level regulations. Money transmission licenses cost $10,000–$50,000+ across multiple states. A Nigerian founder scaled back from 50 states to five key markets, cutting first-year compliance from $100,000+ to roughly $25,000.

Children's Products: CPSC testing and certification — lead, small parts, labeling. Third-party testing costs $1,000–$5,000 per product line.

Penalties for operating without permits include fines, product seizures, and shutdowns. The SBDC and UCF BIP advisors can help identify which regulations are relevant to your business.

rows: Before signing a commercial lease or B2B contracts
Practically required — most clients and landlords demand it
Before providing professional services to U.S. clients
Required by many B2B clients in service industries
When you hire your 4th Florida employee (1st in construction)
Legally required above the employee threshold
When you have a physical office, warehouse, or inventory
Required if you have a commercial lease
If you handle customer data or process payments

questions.stem: A founder selling consulting services to U.S. companies is asked to provide proof of insurance during vendor onboarding. Which two types of insurance are most likely required?
options: Workers' compensation and commercial property insurance
Cyber liability and directors & officers (D&O) insurance
questions.explanation: U.S. companies commonly require vendors to carry general liability (for physical and property risks) and professional liability/E&O (for financial harm from professional services). These are the two most frequently requested insurance types during B2B vendor onboarding.

questions.stem: A founder plans to sell packaged food products in the U.S. through Amazon. She has already registered her LLC and obtained general liability insurance. What regulatory step is she most likely missing?
options: Obtaining FDA-compliant labeling and meeting FDACS requirements before listing products
Filing a trademark for her brand name with the USPTO
Purchasing cyber liability insurance to protect customer payment data
Registering for workers' compensation insurance in Florida
questions.explanation: Food and beverage businesses must comply with FDA (federal) and FDACS (Florida) regulations, including labeling requirements, facility inspections, and potentially product testing. These regulatory requirements must be met before selling food products in the U.S., regardless of the sales channel. Filing a trademark (option B) is important but not a regulatory requirement for food sales. Cyber liability insurance (option C) and workers' comp (option D) are not the most likely missing step for a food product launch.

questions.stem: A founder from Vietnam is preparing to sign her first client contract with a U.S. marketing agency. The agency wants to discuss her proprietary analytics methodology before signing. She has no written agreements in place yet. Which contract should she ask the agency to sign first?
options: A service agreement covering scope, payment terms, and deliverables for the project
A terms-of-service document for her company website
An operating agreement for her LLC defining member responsibilities
A non-disclosure agreement (NDA) to protect her proprietary methodology before sharing details
questions.explanation: Before sharing proprietary information, the founder should have the agency sign an NDA. This protects her analytics methodology during the discussion phase. A service agreement (option A) comes after both parties agree on terms. Terms of service (option B) is for her website users, not a client negotiation. An operating agreement (option C) is an internal LLC document, not relevant to a client relationship.

contentBlocks.title: Exercise: Your Risk Assessment
contentBlocks.content:
List your top 3 business risks in the U.S. market. For each risk, identify:

  1. What type of insurance or legal protection addresses this risk
  2. Whether you need it before you launch or can wait
  3. The approximate cost based on the tables in this lesson

Use this list when you talk to an insurance broker. Having a clear picture of your risks makes the conversation faster and the coverage more accurate.

contentBlocks.title: Key Takeaway
contentBlocks.content: The takeaway is clear: get your contracts in writing — every time, with every partner. Carry general liability insurance from day one ($400–$800/year). If you provide professional services, add E&O coverage. Check whether your industry has specific regulatory requirements before you start operating. Apply these steps early: the costs are far cheaper than lawsuits, fines, or lost contracts. Evaluate your coverage needs now, before a client or landlord asks for proof.
